context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this reflection tbh. i don’t really get the whole development/security thing or how to even start it.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's work through it together. Before we get into the readings or theory — just from your own intuition, what do you think happens to a country's economy or infrastructure when it's in the middle of a violent conflict?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning and feeling lost. I should not give them information or scaffold heavily. Instead, I'll use a Socratic opening question to activate whatever prior knowledge they have and get them thinking about the core relationship the prompt is asking about. Keeping it bite-sized and open-ended.

exchanges.student: idk, stuff gets destroyed and people can’t really work or live normally, but then i get fuzzy on how that connects to “development interventions” before/during/after conflict.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — that's actually the core of it. So let me ask: when you say 'stuff gets destroyed and people can't work,' what kinds of outside actors or efforts do you think might step in to address that? What does 'intervening' in a country's development even look like to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good basic intuition about conflict's effects. Now I should help them bridge from that intuition to the concept of 'development interventions' without defining it for them. A targeted question about what they think 'development interventions' might look like will help them make that connection themselves.

exchanges.student: that’s where i’m stuck tbh, cuz “intervention” feels super broad rn. is it like aid, military, NGOs, gov policy, all of it??
exchanges.tutor: You're actually not as stuck as you think — that breadth you're sensing is real. But here's a more focused question: the prompt asks about the relationship between development interventions and the *factors that drive conflict*. So what do you think might cause conflict in the first place in a developing country context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually on the right track — their instinct that intervention is broad and multi-actor is correct. Rather than confirming or elaborating for them, I should push them to think about what the prompt is specifically asking: the relationship between these interventions and the factors that DRIVE conflict. This will sharpen their focus.

exchanges.student: ngl i can think of like poverty, inequality, weak govt, maybe ethnic tension, but i can’t tell which ones are “drivers” vs just background stuff.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid list. Now here's the key question: looking at what you just listed — poverty, inequality, weak government — do those sound like things that development interventions try to address? What does that tell you about the relationship between development and conflict?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress distinguishing between structural conditions and active drivers. Rather than resolving that distinction for them, I should push them to think about the relationship between the factors they listed and development interventions — this is the heart of the prompt and will help them see the circular/bidirectional nature of the development-conflict relationship.

exchanges.student: i guess yeah, but then it feels circular and that’s what’s messing me up fr. like if development is supposed to fix those things, why does the prompt make it sound like development can also be part of the conflict problem?
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're feeling is exactly what the prompt wants you to explore — good catch. Can you think of any situation, real or hypothetical, where an outside intervention meant to 'help' a country might have actually made tensions worse?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely important insight — the circular/bidirectional relationship between development and conflict. This is actually the crux of the prompt. I should validate this and push them to think of a concrete example where development intervention might have made conflict worse, rather than resolving the tension for them.

exchanges.student: maybe if aid only goes to one region or group? or if rebuilding helps elites first? i’m still not sure how to turn that into an actual reflection tho.
exchanges.tutor: Those are strong examples — hold onto them, they'll be useful. Now the prompt also asks about China's rise as a global power. What do you know or associate with how China operates differently from, say, Western donors or the World Bank when it engages with developing countries?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated two genuinely strong examples of how development interventions can exacerbate conflict drivers. This is good material. Rather than helping them write, I should push them toward the second part of the prompt — China and the transformation of the development-security agenda — since they have enough to start thinking about the first part. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: tbh not much, just like infrastructure loans and maybe less talk about democracy/human rights? but idk if that means it changes the whole security-development thing or not.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a meaningful observation. So if Western donors typically attach conditions around governance or human rights, what does that tell you about what they assume the relationship between security and development is? And does China's approach challenge or reinforce that assumption?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a key distinguishing feature of China's approach — infrastructure-focused aid with fewer political conditions. I should push them to connect this observation to the prompt's specific question about whether this 'portends a transformation of the development-security agenda.' They need to think about what the conventional development-security agenda looks like before they can assess whether China challenges it.

exchanges.student: ugh i’m lost again lol, can u just explain the big picture of the whole prompt first?
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think — look at what you've already figured out: interventions can worsen the factors that drive conflict, China operates with fewer political conditions than Western donors. That's most of the prompt already. Can you try putting those two ideas together in a sentence or two as a starting point for your reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the big picture for them, which would violate the core rules — I should never give the answer directly or do the intellectual work for them. However, I can acknowledge their frustration and redirect them by pointing out that they've actually already figured out most of the big picture through our conversation. I'll summarize what THEY have said back to them as a Socratic move, without adding new content.

exchanges.student: i can’t tell if the prompt wants me to argue for convergence or against it, and that’s making me freeze tbh.
exchanges.tutor: The prompt isn't asking you to find the 'correct' side — it's asking for YOUR reasoned position. Based on what you've already said about interventions potentially worsening conflict, what do YOU think: does pushing for more convergence of security and development make sense or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frozen because they think the prompt requires a single correct position. This is a common freshman misconception about reflection/argumentative writing. I should clarify that a reflection requires their own reasoned stance, not a 'right answer,' and push them to commit to a position based on what they've already figured out in our conversation.

exchanges.student: maybe only sometimes? like it seems useful in emergencies but risky if everything gets treated like a security issue, and now i’m overthinking how to structure the paper.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid, arguable position — conditional convergence with real risks. You have enough to start writing. Rather than planning the structure in the abstract, why not just write your opening sentence right now based on that idea and share it here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced, defensible position — conditional convergence. This is good enough to build a reflection around. They're now shifting anxiety to structure, which is a common avoidance pattern. I should not help with structure or writing, but instead push them to start writing based on what they've developed in our conversation. We're at exchange 9, so I should be pushing toward output.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, when the student asks for the big picture, the tutor summarizes what the student has said but does not provide any method-level feedback on how the student should approach structuring or developing their argument. In Turn 10, the tutor affirms the student's position as 'solid' and 'arguable' but gives no process-level guidance on how to develop or support that position in writing — no advice on how to use evidence, how to structure a reflection, or how to move from intuition to argument.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic technique throughout, consistently drawing ideas out of the student rather than providing answers. The student arrives at a defensible position through their own reasoning. The one notable gap is in meta-learning: the tutor affirms the student's final position but does not offer any process-level guidance on how to develop, evidence, or structure a reflection paper, which is what the student explicitly asks for in Turn 10.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully satisfied: the tutor never gives submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas. Criterion 1.2 is fully satisfied: the tutor builds on each student contribution and uses diagnostic questioning before redirecting. Criterion 1.3 receives a 2-point deduction under 1.3.A.b: in Turn 10, when the student says 'I'm overthinking how to structure the paper,' the tutor responds only by affirming the position and asking for an opening sentence, without any guidance on how to structure a reflection, how to use evidence, or how to move from intuition to argument — feedback that is too vague to improve the student's writing method. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoids repetition, stays on-task, uses appropriately concise turns, and maintains a warm, encouraging tone throughout.
